headline: Dual Legal System Creates 147 Jurisdictional Conflicts Annually
context: The Malaysian Bar Council documented an average of 147 jurisdictional conflicts between civil and Shariah courts annually between 2020 and 2024, primarily in family law matters.

cards.big: 147 court cases per year are stuck between civil and Shariah jurisdiction.
cards.sub: Affected families wait an average of 4.5 years while courts determine which system applies.

cards.big: Article 121(1A) separates civil and Shariah court jurisdiction.
cards.sub: The provision was intended to protect Shariah authority. In practice, it creates jurisdictional gaps.

cards.big: Custody, inheritance, and conversion cases are the primary conflict areas.
cards.sub: When one party is Muslim and the other is not, neither court has clear authority.

cards.big: India's 2018 Supreme Court unified family law jurisdiction for inter-religious disputes.
cards.sub: A single tribunal resolves cases that would otherwise bounce between religious and civil courts.

cards.big: 147 families per year enter a legal system that does not know which court has authority. Justice delayed by design is justice denied by design.
A constitutional amendment creating a special inter-jurisdictional tribunal for mixed-religion family disputes would resolve 147 cases per year that currently have no clear home.
